data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main American indices. This article is part of a weekly series where I bring valuable insights about economics and investments. Honestly, it's very difficult to be pessimistic about the American stock market when you analyze data in a cold and agnostic way.

In this article, I will cover data on corporate results, investment fund data, seasonality, and investor temperament to deal with this challenging market, which is also full of opportunities in my opinion.

Earnings Season Boosts Prices

The 3rd quarter earnings season is approaching its end, and to date, company revenues have grown 8.4%. It is the biggest increase since 2022, and had contributions from all sectors. Stock prices at all-time highs are driven by results, not narratives.

Another piece of information that makes me even more confident in the thesis is represented in the graph below. American stock funds continue to receive investment flows, and the most curious thing is that December tends to be the most promising month in this metric.

About AI Bubble Narratives

In parallel, the cash level of fund managers fell to 3.7%, the lowest level in 15 years. If you find a fund manager giving an interview who believes in an AI bubble, it is quite possible that he is mostly allocated to American stocks despite this.

Speaking of bubbles. Searches for AI bubble reached a new all-time high. But what is this bubble that everyone expects will happen?

Something we can expect, in fact, is growth of the American economy. Estimates from the Atlanta FED's GDPNow model point to growth of 4.2% in Q3.

Psychology and Target

Finally, two charts make me extremely optimistic. Despite the all time high that we see, the fear & greed index point to a lot of fear on the part of investors. As Warren Buffett would say, you should be greedy when others are fearful and fearful when others are greedy.

But what would be the target for the S&P 500? Every week I write articles with relevant insights for the readers of Seeking Alpha, and in these articles I bring metrics to estimate a target for the S&P 500. In this article, we must remember seasonality, or rally at the end of the year. The graph below represents this phenomenon well.

As we can see, statistically we are in an extremely interesting period for new purchases. If we consider level 102, and target at 107, we have an upside of 4.9% and a target of 7064 points for the S&P 500 at the end of the year.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main American indices. Additionally, I offer my recommended stock portfolio to the investor. Year to date, the portfolio has risen 25.1%, while the SPY ETF has risen 14.6%.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

As we know, the thesis has several risks. Every week I bring more risks to my bullish thesis, and it will be no different this week. Although the rally is real, it is concentrated in a few companies. As a reference, 158 shares in the S&P 500 are outperforming the indices; this is the 3rd smallest market amplitude since 1960.

Additionally, unemployment among young people with diplomas is at record levels. There is still no precision to estimate the impact of artificial intelligence on this data, however, despite the economy growing, there is also little amplitude in this indicator.

The CDS of AI companies such as Oracle have soared since July. Apparently investors are actually paying to protect themselves from a supposed artificial intelligence bubble.

Although there are many signs to encourage investors, a lot of selectivity is needed at the current moment, and that is what I seek to provide with my articles and recommended portfolio.

The Bottom Line

Despite several comments from managers about the AI ​​bubble, fund managers have never been so allocated to American stocks. The American economy is hot, based on the Atlanta FED's GDP reading.

The S&P 500 prices are at all time high, and there are fundamentals behind this, as a great proof was the robust earnings season. The most curious thing is that, despite prices at all time high, investors remain scared, perhaps due to AI narratives, which is not consistent with an agnostic analysis of the facts.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main American indices. In my opinion, the risk-return ratio remains attractive with a target of 7064 points for the S&P500, and also for the stocks in the recommended portfolio.

data.title: Merck: Excellence Across The Board
data.description:
Merck (MRK) grabbed my attention because it gained a Strong Buy rating from Seeking Alpha Quant earlier in November. I have never covered this stock or invested in it, and I decided to deep dive into MRK's fundamentals. Indeed, MRK's fundamental and valuation analyses confirmed that this stock certainly deserves attention. With its robust strategic positioning in the rapidly growing oncology drugs industry and diversification across a few other thriving niches, the company is well-positioned to create value for shareholders. At the same time, the stock is very attractively valued and offers a solid 3.2% forward dividend yield. With all that being said, I think that MRK really merits a Strong Buy rating.

Reasons to invest in MRK

The first reason to consider investing in Merck is its status as one of the largest and most innovative pharmaceutical companies in the world. The company's commitment to innovation has historically helped it to develop compelling products with premium pricing, which ensured its strong profitability compared to the sector median. The company's biggest asset is its Keytruda oncology medicine, which generates around half of Merck's consolidated sales and is the best-selling medicine in the world.

Besides oncology treatment, large revenue streams of Merck are Vaccines to prevent infectious diseases (18.5% of the total revenue), Animal Health (9.4%), Hospital Acute Care (5%), Diabetes (4%), and other pharmaceutical areas (cardiovascular, virology, and neuroscience). Thus, Merck's revenue mix is highly diversified, which helps mitigate high concentration risks related to the large proportion of Keytruda in consolidated sales.

Since Merck is a mature pharmaceutical company, its revenue growth is modest compared to companies from rapidly growing industries like IT. However, revenue growth is notably ahead of inflation with almost 5% CAGR over the last decade. In an industry where revenue growth potential is limited due to its size and maturity, cost efficiency is a very important criterion for investors. Merck has been performing well in this respect as its EBITDA's average annual growth rate over the last decade was notably above revenue growth. Taking the 10-year horizon, free cash flow (FCF) growth has been slower than revenue. However, we see that FCF growth by far outpaced revenue growth over the last five years.

We see that driving operating profitability remains the management's priority since the metric (TTM basis) is currently at all-time high of almost 40%. The operating cash generating capacity is also improving the company's Q3 EBITDA of around $8.1 billion was around 45% higher on a YoY basis. I am saying "operating cash generating capacity" and use EBITDA to demonstrate it because I want to emphasize pure operating efficiency on cash basis that omits changes in working capital.

Another important bullish aspect is that the management allocates this improving operating cash generation very prudently. The company ended Q3 with $18 billion in cash and both key liquidity (current and quick) ratios above 1. Leverage level is also modest with a $41 billion total debt compared to the $260 billion market cap. I am emphasizing strength of Merck's balance sheet because it is a vital foundation for the future growth. Merck's growth strategy is multifaceted, meaning investments in internal growth projects (R&D) as well as growth via strategic acquisitions (M&A). With its solid financial flexibility, MRK is well-equipped to invest in growth. The company's historical average return on total capital of above 15% means there is a proven record of successful investments in the past.

Besides financing growth initiatives, Merck's robust financial position is also important because of the company's status of dividend consistency and growth champion. The forward dividend yield of 3.2% is also accompanied by quite frequent share buybacks. With such a strong balance between investing in growth, maintaining financial stability, and ensuring shareholder returns, I think that the demand for MRK stock is poised to remain stable. This is a crucial factor to maintain steady share price growth.

Last but not least, Merck's valuation ratios look really good at the moment. Most of the key valuation ratios are significantly lower than Merck's historical averages. In addition, Merck's multiples are way below the sector median despite its stellar profitability compared to peer averages and still expanding profitability metrics. Merck's low multiples does not look fair given its clean balance sheet and the management's commitment to drive dividend growth and conduct share buybacks.

Reasons to avoid MRK

MRK gained around 6% YTD, which is weaker performance compared to the broader U.S. equity market. Such underperformance is partially explained by the overall cautious sentiment around pharmaceuticals as the industry faces substantial political and regulatory uncertainty due to President Trump's initiatives in these area (drugs' price ceilings, tariffs related to raw materials for pharmaceuticals). Adverse regulatory developments might undermine Merck's profitability because any price ceiling limits a business's pricing power, while increased tariffs will make sourcing materials more expensive. With potential intensified price regulation, it is highly unlikely that MRK will be able to pass on elevated tariffs' effect to end customers.

Additionally, having a superstar product like Keytruda is a two-edged sword. On the one hand, a product that dominates the entire pharmaceutical market underscores Merck's excellence as an innovative drugmaker. On the other hand, having one drug generating half of a company's sales is extremely risky because its patent exclusivity is not infinite. Keytruda is expected to lose its U.S. patent exclusivity in 2028, meaning that the product will face increased competition from generics within the next few years. Of course, Merck is acting proactively to ensure that it will not be a sharp Keytruda revenue decline by implementing lifecycle strategies like the subcutaneous Keytruda Qlex launch. Therefore, Keytruda's loss of patent exclusivity will be a "soft-landing" revenue-wise, but it will still be a "landing".

It might be the Keytruda patent cliff risk as the main reason why Wall Street analysts do not think that MRK is undervalued. Their target price is almost identical to the current market price, meaning they see no upside potential.

The verdict

I think that MRK is a compelling investment opportunity because it is a well-rounded business. The company invests in growth and innovation while paying dividends with share buybacks while maintaining a healthy financial position. The multiples look unfairly low across the board, making MRK a Strong Buy investment opportunity.

data.title: Enovis: MedTech Play Is Far From Healthy
data.description:
In the spring of 2024 I concluded that Enovis (ENOV) was posting solid growth, yet it found itself on an unstable financial footing. The MedTech business, formerly known as Colfax, had quite some to prove after the welding business, ESAB (ESAB) was spun off from the business.

This medical business largely originated from a $3.1 billion acquisition of DJO Global in 2018 and with many questioning the rationale of having a welding and MedTech business under a single roof. This skepticism ended up being the basis to separate the business again many years later.

About Enovis

Enovis is a $2.1 billion ortho business that reports its results across two equally large divisions. These are the Recon businesses, which stand for reconstructive, as well as prevention and recovery businesses, also known as P&R.

The reconstructive business is showing relatively stronger growth, among others active in extremities such as shoulder, knee, recovery services, and foot & ankle. The prevention & recovery business focuses on sports medicine bracing, offering a wide range of such products.

Some 60% of sales are generated in the US, with the remainder of revenues generated abroad. These sales grow at a mid-single-digit comparable sales growth rate, with adjusted EBITDA margins reported in the high-teens.

The business is active across the entire ortho spectrum, ranging from prevention, to repair, to recovery and rehabilitation. The business has been separated since April 2022, with shares at the time trading in the $60s. Ever since, shares have gradually fallen to the $30 mark here, as the actual cold hard results have been lagging rosier adjusted numbers by a wide margin.

Back To 2024

Shares of the company traded at the $52 mark in April 2024 as there were quite a few moving targets. Following the separation, the company announced an EUR 800 million deal to acquire Italian-based LimaCorporate S.p.a. in September 2023, a deal seen as boosting sales by nearly $300 million.

The company ended up growing 2023 sales by 9% to $1.70 billion, as organic growth came in at a solid 8%. Adjusted earnings were reported at $2.40 per share, while a GAAP loss of $1.00 per share was reported at the same time.

The company guided for 2024 sales to advance to $2.10 billion, with EBITDA seen around $365-$380 million. That was badly needed with net debt reported at $430 million; that is ahead of the purchase of LimaCorporate, which made that net debt rise to $1.27 billion upon closing, for a 3.4 times leverage ratio. Adjusted earnings for 2024 were seen around $2.50 per share, but again, these were highly adjusted.

Trading at $52 per share, the enterprise valuation came in at $4.1 billion, which looks rather disappointing after the company acquired DJO Global in a $3.1 billion deal in 2018, while another billion in M&A deals were announced ever since.

While a 2 times sales multiple was modest, the 20 times adjusted earnings multiple felt rich, mostly because of the highly adjusted nature of these earnings. This made shares a show-me story, as I disliked the aggressive accounting and leverage practices.

Coming Down

In the one and a half years that followed, shares have seen 40% declines to current levels at $30 per share. This came even after the company continued to grow, with 2024 sales reported up 23% to $2.10 billion, supported by a solid 6% organic increase in sales.

Adjusted earnings rose by forty-four cents to $2.84 per share, with adjusted earnings reported at $158 million. Again, these earnings are highly adjusted, including a $30 million stock-based compensation expense, $78 million in strategic transaction costs, $45 million in restructuring charges, and others, including a $645 million goodwill impairment charge.

Adjusted for these and other items, adjusted EBITDA was reported at $376 million, concerning as net debt ticked up to $1.28 billion, indicating that the highly adjusted earnings are really highly adjusted, with net debt ticking up further.

The company guided for 2025 sales to advance to $2.19-$2.22 billion, with organic growth seen at 6.0-6.5%, EBITDA was seen around $410 million, and adjusted earnings were seen between $3.10-$3.25 per share. The arrival of tariffs made it so that the outlook was hiked alongside the first quarter earnings report, yet earnings metrics were seen under pressure.

The guidance was hiked across the board following the release of the second quarter results, and in October, Enovis announced a bolt-on divestment. The company reached a deal to sell the Dr. Comfort diabetic shoe business in a $60 million cash deal, consisting of a $45 million upfront cash payment and the remainder in the form of an earn-out. Unfortunately, few financial details on the divested activities have been announced.

Early in November, Enovis reported third quarter results as the full year guidance was updated again in a smaller way. Full year sales are now seen between $2.24-$2.27 billion, reflective of tariffs, with EBITDA seen at a midpoint of $400 million. This results in earnings seen between $3.10 and $3.25 per share.

Net debt remains high and actually increased to $1.32 billion, which still makes that leverage seen over 3 times, as cash flow generation remains soft, amidst continued highly adjusted metrics. This comes amidst continued charges, as the company incurred another $548 million goodwill impairment charge.

And Now?

Based on a share count of 57 million shares trading at the $30 mark, the market value of the firm stands at $1.7 billion, for a $3.0 billion enterprise valuation here. In between, the money spent on the purchase of DJO Global as well as M&A efforts thereafter suggests that a billion in value has gone up in smoke here.

This is driven by the illusion of high profits, but realistic profits are hurt by many substantial items, including items such as Medical Device Regulation expenses, restructuring charges, strategic transaction costs, stock-based compensation expenses, and others.

This is the big issue. While organic growth looks decent, assets look decent, and sales multiples are low, the issue is that of profitability, or better said, lack thereof. While the company reports impressive adjusted earnings numbers, realistic earnings are still pretty much nonexistent, an issue going on for years now.

Frankly, shares deserve a closer look, as they trade at just 1.3 times sales here, which is a very non-demanding multiple for an industry that typically trades at 4-5 times sales in many cases. The real issue is that of margins and debt, which are closely linked, as margins look good on paper, but heavy adjustments simply have to diminish in the future in order to create appeal and a credible roadmap for shares to make a recovery going ahead.

data.title: IREN Limited: A Speculative Gem In The AI Cloud Landscape
data.description:
IREN Limited (NASDAQ: IREN), formerly known as Iris Energy, began as a Bitcoin mining company. However, recently, investors have become fascinated by the company's successful pivot in late 2024/early 2025 to a renewable-powered AI cloud and data center company. The company uses an Infrastructure as a Service ("IaaS") business model. Be aware that not every company classified as an IaaS performs the same services.

For instance, CoreWeave (CRWV) is an IaaS that provides a customer experience similar to a traditional cloud provider (like AWS or GCP), only with a focus on storage, networking, and raw compute via managed NVIDIA GPU-based bare-metal servers. It also offers managed software services, such as Kubernetes. It operates like a customer signing up for AWS, GCP, or Azure. It provides the customer with a cloud dashboard and API access. The customer uploads their code, and the CoreWeave platform handles the orchestration and deployment onto the physical servers. As a result, you can classify CoreWeave as a GPU-as-a-Service business (AI Cloud).

In contrast, IREN's IaaS business is different. IREN builds data centers, rents data center buildings, and gives customers control over the physical server farm (colocation). It also provides power, power connections, storage, a bare-metal GPU server, and networking equipment, such as InfiniBand. The customer is responsible for installing their own operating system, application software, and deployment tools on the server.

One significant difference between CoreWeave and IREN is that IREN focuses solely on data center hardware, while avoiding software services. IREN doesn't want anything to do with software. IREN's customers tend to be highly sophisticated (Often hyperscalers) who prefer to choose and install the software themselves.

I have written several articles over the past month covering CoreWeave and Nebius (NBIS), and now IREN. Think of it this way: IREN is an all-hardware and physical infrastructure IaaS provider, Coreweave is bare-metal hardware IaaS with some software, and Nebius is a full-stack IaaS, platform-as-a-service ("PaaS"), and software-as-a-service ("SaaS") provider.

Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Daniel Roberts said on the IREN first quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2026 earnings call:

We develop our own greenfield sites, engineer our own high-voltage infrastructure, build and operate our own data centers and deploy our own GPUs. Simply put, we control the entire stack from the substation all the way down to the GPU. We believe strongly that this end-to-end integration and control is a key differentiator that positions us for significant growth.

Another distinction between CoreWeave and IREN that is becoming less of one is that IREN builds and owns its data center. At the same time, CoreWeave has historically leased data centers from third-party developers, including Core Scientific (CORZ) and Applied Digital (APLD). The weakness in the leasing model is that it exposes CoreWeave to execution risk from third parties. In my last article on CoreWeave, I discussed how a third party experienced a delay in bringing a CoreWeave data center online. CoreWeave now recognizes the weakness of relying on third parties and is rapidly shifting to a data center ownership model similar to IREN.

Now, let's discuss the stock's performance. IREN began moving higher in late June or early July this year, then spiked from late August to its 52-week high of $76.87 on November 5, 2025. As of November 21, the stock is up around 330.3% year to date to $42.26.

The stock has recently dropped amid profit-taking after a massive run since the summer. Even before the company's first-quarter FY 2026 earnings on November 6, the market was shaky about IREN and the broader Artificial Intelligence ("AI") infrastructure market, amid fears of an AI bubble popping.

The market also has significant fears about IREN's balance sheet. The company has $1.032 billion in cash and short-term investments and $965.23 million in long-term debt.

Despite the massive rise in debt in 2025, the company's debt-to-equity ratio is only 0.34, indicating it currently conservatively funds its operations with equity rather than debt. Its first-quarter FY 2026 trailing 12-month ("TTM") debt-to-EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) is 1.0, calculated by dividing total debt of $965.6 million by its EBITDA over the last four quarters of $959.6 million. Since different websites calculate EBITDA differently, I calculated EBITDA by adding the company-supplied EBITDA figures for the previous four quarters, as shown on page 22 of IREN's first-quarter FY 2026 investor presentation. The market generally considers a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 1.0 as healthy. If its balance sheet looks pristine, then why are investors concerned about IREN's balance sheet?

The answer is that the market is not concerned about how the balance sheet looks today, but what it may look like two or three years from now. Although the company's balance sheet looks healthy for now, after management fully funds the CapEx for its numerous projects, it might not look so healthy. When all is said and done, IREN will likely need to take on substantial new debt, issue new equity, or both, which will drastically increase the Debt-to-Equity and Debt-to-EBITDA ratios going forward and potentially dilute existing investors.

New debt adds to its execution risk. Forward-thinking investors are concerned about the potential for the company to encounter problems generating revenue from its projects due to construction delays, GPU supply chain issues, insufficient power sources for AI data centers, or customers delaying the onboarding of projects, while simultaneously carrying a significant debt load to fund the project on the balance sheet. Any future revenue delays could reduce EBITDA, resulting in a higher Debt-to-EBITDA ratio and creating a considerable risk of the company running into financial problems or even defaulting on a loan if the ratio rises too quickly. How investors value the stock is highly sensitive to declines in the company's EBITDA.

As a result, investors also weren't exactly thrilled with the company's first-quarter FY 2026 earnings.

The company's first-quarter FY 2026 adjusted EBITDA margin decreased by 27 points sequentially to 38%, which some might interpret as an indication of operational issues. Page 19 of the company's first quarter FY 2026 investor presentation contains the following commentary about the reason for the EBITDA decline: "$85.1m increase in SG&A primarily driven by a materially higher share price, resulting in an additional $(23.9)m of accelerated stock-based amortization from achievement of share price targets, and by $(32.8) m of payroll taxes accrued on the market value of RSUs."

Note the $32.8 million payroll tax expense in the quote above because it is the main cause of the lower Adjusted EBITDA margin. Although the reason for the increased payroll tax is unlikely to recur anytime soon, and the core EBITDA margin is healthy and shows nothing to be concerned about, some investors may have adopted a shoot-first-and-ask-questions-later mindset toward this company due to its perceived high risk. No one wants to be left holding the bag if something should go wrong, and the psychological impact of seeing the massive decline in adjusted EBITDA margin was no doubt at least partially responsible for the stock sinking 6.8% the day after the company released earnings.

Despite the company's high risk, there are reasons some investors remain interested in the stock. One of the biggest reasons is that the company's plan to date has produced blistering revenue growth. CFO Lewis said on the first quarter earnings call, "Q1 FY'26 marked our fifth consecutive quarter of record revenues with total revenue reaching $240 million, up 20% -- 28% quarter-over-quarter and 355% year-over-year."

The company also hinted on the earnings call that there is massive, explosive revenue growth from AI moving forward. Because management has discussed AI so much lately on earnings calls, some may not realize that 97% of the company's revenue still comes from Bitcoin mining.

Management likely doesn't want to highlight that fact because it draws attention to the massive scale its AI business needs to achieve over the next year for the investment to pay off, which reminds investors that many things could go wrong.

More On IREN's GPU Expansion And Microsoft

IREN's shift from Bitcoin mining to an AI infrastructure provider has been on the table for the past two years. The company began the shift with its first GPU purchase in August 2023. However, the market didn't take the company seriously until it announced on March 31, 2025, that it had paused expansion of its Bitcoin operations to focus on its AI infrastructure business.

However, well into mid-summer, the market's focus on this company still revolved around Bitcoin pricing, probably because the company had yet to announce any earth-moving AI infrastructure deals.

During IREN's fourth-quarter FY 2025 earnings call on August 28, CEO Roberts announced that it had brought "10,000 GPUs online or being commissioned in the coming months." It was around late August that the market truly began to pay attention to its AI program, and the stock took off. Here I am going to point out something that you should note when reading future announcements from the company. More often than not, this company talks in hypotheticals. The CEO didn't say there were 10,000 GPUs online today. He said some are "being commissioned in the coming months." This language is important because if the company has execution issues, it may not reach the number of GPUs the CEO mentions.

On October 7, the company announced the following:

IREN Limited (NASDAQ: IREN) ("IREN") today announced it has signed additional multi-year cloud services contracts with leading AI companies for NVIDIA Blackwell GPU deployments. IREN recently expanded its AI Cloud capacity and remains on track to achieve >$500m in annualized run-rate revenue ("ARR") from the 23k GPUs currently operating and on order by the end of Q1 2026.

I misread the above statement on my first read. Initially, I thought the statement meant that the company's ARR would be at $500 million by the end of the first quarter of FY 2026. Upon second reading, I saw my first interpretation was in error. The press release is more aspirational and indicated that, at the end of the first quarter, the company was on track to reach more than $500 million in ARR once its first 23,000 GPUs are fully operational.

At the time of the press release, many of those GPUs may not have been online yet, and some may still have been on order. So, investors shouldn't have assumed that the company was definitely going to be at $500 million in ARR at the end of the first quarter.

Among the reasons some find this company risky is that management can sometimes be vague or omit seemingly important information from its company communications, especially regarding timelines and growth rates for some projects. Part of the reason for the vagueness is that the company likely doesn't know the precise timelines itself because of the potential for delays.

In this case, the company did reach its 23,000 GPU and $500 million ARR goal. CEO Roberts said on the first quarter FY 2026 earnings call, "When combined with the $1.9 billion expected from the Microsoft contract and $500 million from our existing 23,000 GPU deployment, this expansion provides a clear pathway to approximately $3.4 billion in total annualized run rate revenue once fully ramped."

As of now, the company is still on schedule to grow its GPU fleet from 23,000 GPUs at the end of the first-quarter FY 2026 call to 140,000 GPUs. Management expects that, when it fully deploys all 140,000 GPUs, it will generate around $3.4 billion in annualized run-rate revenue ("ARR") while using only 16% of its binding contracts with grid operators for power capacity of three gigawatts. So, even with the rapid data center expansion it has planned through the end of 2026, IREN still has significant capacity to power more data centers, which is important, since one of the biggest roadblocks to bringing more data centers online is finding power for them.

An IREN press release on November 3 stated that the company signed a five-year, $9.7 billion contract with Microsoft, which the company estimates will account for 57% of its FY 2026 ARR guidance, or $1.9 billion. Microsoft was the type of whale hyperscaler that the market hoped would sign with IREN.

Since IREN's pivot to the cloud and AI has become a leading thesis for IREN bulls, the market should move higher on news of IREN signing deals with any leading cloud providers. The catalyst for the stock's move to a 52-week-high in early November was this Microsoft deal.

The deal validates the company's decision to move away from the volatile Bitcoin mining business and toward providing cloud computing and AI infrastructure to hyperscalers, a more stable, long-term revenue stream.

I included the following image from the company first quarter investor presentation because it highlights in green the physical locations of IREN's AI Cloud contracted to Microsoft in Canada and in the U.S. Sometimes during earnings calls, management discusses the sites at Mackenzie, British Columbia (B.C.), Prince George, B.C., and Canal Flats, B.C. in Canada and Childress, Texas and Sweetwater, Texas in the U.S.

One last thing to note about the Microsoft deal is that the hyperscaler made a 20% prepayment, valued at approximately $1.94 billion, to fund the AI cloud CapEx. The prepayment provides a massive, non-dilutive source of capital that substantially relieves IREN of the immediate pressure to raise funds via equity or debt.

However, the prepayment only delays the pressure to raise funds. Eventually, the company will burn through the $1.94 billion prepayment, and when management announces a future capital raise, the market may react negatively. As a result, expect this stock to be highly volatile as the market's mood vacillates between exuberance over positive news about signing new deals and deploying GPUs and negative news, such as potential project delays or problems raising capital.

Risks

Even with the potential for high revenue growth down the road, the company's valuation is highly sensitive to its capital raises. IREN last raised capital on October 14, issuing $1 billion in potentially dilutive convertible notes. The market wasn't thrilled. The stock dropped by 25.48% from $69.56 on October 14 to close at $51.83 on October 22. So, if you invest in this stock, be aware that it can become volatile around capital raises. Given the capital expenditures ("CapEX") this company will need over the next several years, expect further capital raises to fuel the growth engine. The market may not necessarily like the near-term repercussions of future capital raises.

Some of IREN's projects, like the Sweetwater and the Childress Horizons site, are immensely complex. Management has an aggressive plan to expand its GPU fleet from 23,000 to 140,000 GPUs over a very short period.

Management stated on Page 17 of its first quarter FY 2026 investor presentation:

ARR of $3.4bn represents expected $1.9bn average annual revenue under Microsoft (MSFT) contract plus estimated $1.5bn ARR from potential ~63k GPU deployment at British Columbia sites, based on internal company assumptions regarding GPU models, utilization and pricing. It is not fully contracted, there can be no assurance that it will be achieved, and actual revenue may differ materially. Assumes on time delivery and commissioning of GPUs.

As the above quote indicates, management provided a timeline to reach $3.4 billion in ARR by the end of calendar year 2026, with $1.9 billion in ARR supposed to come from Microsoft. However, note that management makes several assumptions to reach those figures and doesn't guarantee anything. Its ARR figures carry significant execution risk. If the company encounters significant delays in site development, grid connection approvals, or infrastructure buildout, it could impede revenue recognition, and EBITDA could decline enough to raise investor concerns about the company's financial stability.

Another thing that could halt IREN's ability to bring data centers online in its tracks is a lack of enough GPUs. Although the company gained NVIDIA Preferred Partner Status (PPP) on August 28, 2025, it doesn't source all its GPUs from NVIDIA; it sources them through third parties. For instance, IREN sources GPUs for the Microsoft project through Dell Technologies (DELL). If Dell is unable to secure enough GPUs for the AI infrastructure provider for any reason, the company's data center projects could quickly stall, despite IREN's NVIDIA PPP.

The market has based much of IREN's current valuation on successfully reaching its $3.4 billion ARR target. Investors are overly sensitive to any perceived operational missteps or financing hiccups that threaten its ability to meet its ARR target. The stock's sharp sell-offs following its recent convertible note issuance and the decline in first-quarter EBITDA margin show that the market closely monitors the company's financing and operations.

Valuation

The following chart compares IREN with several peers in AI infrastructure.

IREN's trailing 12-month TTM P/S ratio of 16.48 is below my calculation of the group's median TTM P/S ratio of 17.30, suggesting the market may undervalue it compared to its peers. I excluded IREN from my median calculation to ensure it would not influence the median I compare it against.

Analysts estimate IREN's EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) will grow from $708.15 million in FY 2026 to $2.953 billion in FY 2028, a 103.84% compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") increase.

The following compares IREN's forward enterprise value ("EV") to EBITDA (EV/EBITDA), analysts' estimated two-year EBITDA CAGR, and TTM EBITDA margin. One of the better ways to perform a relative valuation of capital-intensive manufacturing companies is through EV/EBITDA, which provides a standardized view of each company's core operating performance.

Source of data in table: YCharts

The Forward EV/EBITDA to two-year EBITDA growth ratio operates under the same rules as the P/S/G ratio, meaning the market considers a company trading at a lower ratio to be undervalued compared to one trading at a higher ratio.

The market would likely consider IREN undervalued compared to TeraWulf and Applied Digital for two reasons. IREN (0.16) has a lower Forward EV/EBITDA-to-two-year EBITDA growth ratio than TeraWulf (0.39) and Applied Digital (0.48). Additionally, despite TeraWulf and Applied Digital companies growing EBITDA much faster, both companies have negative EBITDA margins.

Since IREN (33.56%) has the second-highest EBITDA margin behind CoreWeave (50.04%), IREN likely deserves a premium over all companies in the comparison, except CoreWeave. As a result, I consider IREN undervalued to all but CoreWeave in this EV/EBITDA comparison.

However, in this valuation comparison and others in this article, be aware that I am keeping the analysis very simple by considering only the raw, static numbers. Other factors, such as where EBITDA margins can go in the future, how much companies will spend on future CapEx, dilution risk, and other risks or benefits, could change my assessment of the comparisons between companies. For instance, if IREN requires significantly more CapEx to grow EBITDA by 103% over the next two years than TeraWulf/Applied Digital does for its growth, the market may correctly discount IREN.

The following table compares IREN's two-year Forward P/S ratio to estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) and TTM profitability metrics. Usually, I use P/S/G to perform a relative valuation. However, on an absolute basis, the market may consider all the companies in the table undervalued, as it generally views P/S/G below 1.0 as undervalued. However, the AI infrastructure industry's low P/S/G ratio suggests the market believes the risk is high that all of these companies may be unable to execute on their revenue growth opportunity, and has deeply discounted their valuation relative to the overall market.

The market may justify all of these companies trading below one because of the industry's high CapEx intensity, and it views them as far riskier than non-capital-intensive companies with high-growth rates. Therefore, given the high execution risk, some investors may consider all of the following AI infrastructure stocks fairly valued, despite their high revenue growth rates.

IREN trades at a considerable discount to the P/S/G premiums of TeraWulf and Applied Digital. While also trading at a premium to stocks at the lower end of the P/S/G spectrum. As a result, some may consider IREN fairly valued based solely on relative P/S/G.

Factoring in profitability makes the assessment more complex and highlights each company's relative risks. In this comparison, I will also consider figures from the EV/EBITDA table.

When considering all the numerical qualitative factors, CoreWeave and IREN present the lowest risk based on today's static metrics.

IREN has the second-best gross margin, solid triple-digit estimated EBITDA growth over the next two years, and an EV/EBITDA ratio below or nearly equal to that of most of its high-revenue-growth peers (Nebius, CoreWeave, WhiteFiber, Applied Digital, TeraWulf). The one issue that may worry IREN investors is how future capital raises will affect the balance sheet. The one offsetting factor to future balance sheet risk is Microsoft's 20% prepayment under its contract, which pushes the next capital raise further out.

CoreWeave also has a lower risk rating than the others due to the second-lowest forward P/S (indicating the market has factored in the risk of investing in AI infrastructure better than its peers), the highest gross margin, and the third-highest forward estimated EBITDA CAGR. Like all the others, it has execution risk. Still, the two-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth valuation is relatively modest compared to IREN, Hive Digital, Applied Digital, and TeraWulf.

On the other end of the risk spectrum are Nebius, WhiteFiber, Applied Digital, TeraWulf, which are essentially "venture capital" bets trading in the public markets. All of the companies have negative FCF margins, but the worst are Nebius (-812.49%), Applied Digital (-574.71%), TeraWulf (-403.64%), and WhiteFiber (-348.43%). To make things even worse for Terawulf and Applied Digital, they have the highest and second-highest Forward EV/EBITDA ratios, respectively, which suggests the market may not have fully factored in the risk of investing in them. All of these companies have high execution risk with little room for error. Yet, none of these "venture capital" bets may have priced in the risk.

Considering all these factors, I believe the market undervalues IREN relative to the group. However, on an absolute basis, some investors may consider it overvalued compared to other high-growth stocks in the market with perceived less risk, such as NVIDIA or Palantir (PLTR).

IREN has a price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio of 21.25, below the information Technology ("IT") sector of 28.56, a sign of undervaluation.

The company's FY 2026 forward P/E to analysts' estimated EPS growth ("PEG") ratio is 0.23, calculated by dividing its forward P/E of 39.70 by analysts' estimated EPS growth of 172.92%. Generally, the market considers a PEG ratio below 1.0 as a sign of undervaluation. Suppose the stock sold at a PEG ratio of 1.0 today, its price would be $183.30, a 279% rise over its November 24 intraday price of $48.36.

However, the market will likely not price that EPS growth rate in because analysts don't expect the company to maintain it. Analysts estimate EPS will grow only 15.32% in FY 2027, then return to triple-digit growth in FY 2028 (129.67%), before decelerating in FY 2029. Since the market expects inconsistent EPS growth over the next five years, calculating a price target based on forward EPS growth estimates could yield erroneous results.

Wall Street analysts have set a consensus price target of $81.38 for IREN, a 68.3% increase from its November 24 intraday price. However, this stock is speculative and likely to be very volatile, reacting sharply in either direction to both good and bad news. The price target range is $24 to $136, a widespread that shows significant disagreement about the company's fortunes. My advice is to be cautious about placing too much weight on price targets for this stock from any analysts, as there is a significant chance they will be wrong.

IREN Is A Buy For Aggressive Growth Investors Only

This type of stock will appeal only to risk-tolerant investors who are confident in their assessment of the stock and the industry. The main problem some will have with IREN is that it is tough to value, as the company is very early in the middle of a transformation from a Bitcoin miner to an AI infrastructure provider.

The reason some analysts still have low price targets for the stock is that they still view it as a Bitcoin miner. The possibility still exists that management will discover the AI infrastructure business is not paying off and eventually back away from the industry. As a result, some investors don't yet see much value in the AI infrastructure business.

There is little history of this company operating primarily as an AI infrastructure provider on the market. No one has a history of management's ability to execute at scale in this market, which is why some consider execution one of the most significant risks. Running an AI infrastructure site for the likes of Microsoft, which guarantees 99.99% uptime on several of its cloud services, is far different from operating a Bitcoin mining operation, where there are few consequences for occasional power outages, network issues, and hardware failures.

Additionally, much of the AI infrastructure market is new, and there is little historical precedent for building AI data centers from scratch and renting AI services. Several of IREN's peers, such as CoreWeave, are former Bitcoin miners that have transitioned to AI infrastructure providers, and others, like TeraWulf, are current Bitcoin Miners in the middle of that transition.

All analysts will likely have challenges creating an accurate model to value this company. If you are the type of investor who relies on analysts presenting you with accurate valuations and precise price targets, IREN may be a disappointing investment for you. Analysts aren't the only ones who will likely have a hard time forecasting this company's future. I suspect that management doesn't know how it will all play out either. Conservative investors and value investors would be better off staying far away from what will likely be a very volatile stock.

However, aggressive growth investors looking for solid market-beating potential should consider IREN. This stock will likely move mostly in line with investor sentiment. Suppose sentiment toward AI investments is positive, and there is positive news that the company is on or ahead of schedule to reach $3.4 billion in ARR by the end of calendar year 2026. In that case, this stock will likely head up, no matter what a valuation formula spits out. An investment in this stock assumes that management can execute its vision. If management executes, the stock will likely be a market beater. If management fails to implement its vision, then a significant downside is on the table.

If you are an aggressive growth investor with a high-risk tolerance, consider investing in this speculative stock today. I rate the stock a buy, but only for investors willing to speculate with a small portion of a diversified portfolio.

data.title: Whale's Tracking - Deepening Rift
data.description:
Examining the Fed's Internal Divisions

The Fed's schism stems fundamentally from differing interpretations of economic metrics: inflation paths, labour market indicators, policy trajectories, and approaches to data gaps. With core PCE inflation hovering around 3%—exceeding the 2% target—hawks perceive it as a latent rebound risk, amplified by escalating trade tariff uncertainties; doves maintain that inflation has moderated sufficiently to warrant additional easing. Both sides recognise the employment slowdown (September yielded only 119,000 jobs, below expectations).

Yet, hawks deem it insufficient to warrant immediate intervention, whereas doves fear it could cascade into broader economic fragility. On the policy front, hawks advocate a pause or measured adjustments from the prevailing 3.75%-4.00% federal funds range; doves press for a December cut, possibly extending to 50 bps. The data blackout heightens frictions: doves contend that available evidence is adequate for action, while hawks emphasise prudence to mitigate blind spots.

Statements from prominent figures in November delineate the split. Dovish advocates include Fed Governor Christopher Waller, who stresses labour market vulnerabilities, arguing that existing data justifies further cuts and downplays the blackout's impact—his November 25 remarks elevated the December cut probabilities to 65%-80%. New York Fed President John Williams supports easing, viewing labour cooling as a vital warning; his late-November comments boosted market odds to 69%. Fed Governor Stephen Miran calls for decisive action, advocating at least 50 basis points to forestall recession, observing in his 25 November statement that inflation remains persistent but employment is weakening more rapidly.

Hawks counter: Kansas City Fed President Jeffrey Schmid regards inflation as excessively high for adjustments, deeming October's cut superfluous; he reaffirmed on November 14 that progress could falter. Dallas Fed's Lorie Logan concurs that the October easing exceeded the requirements, urging caution to avoid over-stimulation—her discussions on 14 and 21 November highlight the constrained leeway for further moves. Cleveland Fed's Beth Hammack prioritises inflation risks, moderating December cut expectations in her comments on November 14. St. Louis Fed's Alberto Musalem urges circumspection with entrenched 3% inflation, prioritising dual-mandate equilibrium; his early and late-November cautions underscore the hazards of excessive easing.

Moderately hawk-leaning centrists include the Chicago Fed's Austan Goolsbee, who perceives inflation pressures as potentially delayed but employment deterioration as accelerating, having transitioned from a dovish stance in early November. Chair Jerome Powell adopts a neutral posture, facilitating potential cuts through allies while remaining data-contingent; his late-November tone, bolstered by individuals like Waller, maintains adaptability.

This dissonance is evident in October's FOMC minutes, where officials diverged sharply on sustained cuts—some suggesting a year-end suspension. Markets have fluctuated accordingly: the December cut odds dropped to 40% early in November, rebounding to 80% or higher amid dovish cues. On X, the contention is apparent, with Polymarket odds climbing to 87%.

Outside the Fed, analysts are providing valuable context, highlighting the implications of the rift. Forbes' Simon Moore observes that the October minutes' hawkish inflection depressed cut probabilities, exposing "profound disagreements." Markets.com dissects 2025 FOMC outlooks: doves emphasise employment vulnerabilities, and hawks inflation revival. Fortune's Siegel construes Williams' receptiveness as dovish preparation, contrasting hawks' insistence on data inadequacy. Reuters warns that conflicting views are intensifying market wager volatility, positioning December as a "contentious episode." Nuveen's investment perspective interprets Powell's press conference as hawk-leaning, recalibrating cut anticipations. BCA Research posits that the minutes reveal hawk-dove profundity, enhancing pause prospects. FXStreet notes officials' judicious yet fragmented path evaluation.

The prevailing view? Data delays and dual-mandate strains engender decision-making intrigue.

Potential Market Repercussions from the Divisions

The Fed's divisions not only amplify policy ambiguity but could also induce substantial financial market oscillations.

  • If doves prevail and a cut materialises, equities stand to gain, particularly in growth and technology segments (Nasdaq has advanced 2.5% on dovish rhetoric), with the S&P 500 potentially advancing 1-2%. Historical easing phases have delivered average equity returns of 10-15% (as seen in 2023-2024). Bond yields would recede, with 10-year Treasuries falling below 4%, which would benefit REITs and ETFs such as TLT and VNQ by approximately 2%. The dollar would depreciate (DXY repelled at 100), bolstering gold (reaching two-week highs, eyeing $4,190-4,245). Cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin might surge. In sum, recession probabilities would diminish, albeit with risks of subsequent adjustments if inflation reaccelerates. JPMorgan has revised its stance to endorse a cut.

  • Conversely, a hawk-dominated pause elevates retrenchment hazards. The S&P 500 could retreat to 4,800 (mirroring 20 November's $1 trillion market capitalisation erosion). AI leaders, such as Nvidia, would likely encounter heightened post-earnings volatility. Rising yields would escalate borrowing expenses, constraining REITs and fixed-income instruments. Dollar appreciation would suppress commodities (gold and crypto declining). Hawkish indicators have historically propelled VIX spikes of 20-30%. Wider ramifications: heightened inflation and tariff apprehensions fostering risk aversion, with commodities already subdued by hawkish undertones.

Ambiguity itself magnifies impacts; over the longer horizon, 2026 policy may ease (hawks frequently yield to doves), but near-term fissures could engender "sugar-rush" economic patterns—swift easing succeeded by abrupt corrections. Continued employment erosion (e.g., Verizon's 13,000 redundancies) would fortify dovish positions.

Bottom Line

The Fed's split stems from a data drought and a mandate on a tightrope, with markets pricing in a dovish tilt—but fresh numbers or speeches could flip the script. It is clear that this isn't policy as usual; it's a test of resolve shaping global finance. Traders keep a close eye on the charts next week and use them to navigate the turbulence. The Fed's moves—or lack thereof—will echo far beyond the Eccles Building.
